# OpenAI Model Pricing Summary

## Current Text Models

**GPT-5 Series:**
- **GPT-5.4** (Standard): $2.50 input / $15.00 output per 1M tokens
- **GPT-5.4-pro** (Standard): $30.00 input / $180.00 output per 1M tokens
- **GPT-5.2** (Standard): $1.75 input / $14.00 output per 1M tokens
- **GPT-5.1** (Standard): $1.25 input / $10.00 output per 1M tokens
- **GPT-5** (Standard): $1.25 input / $10.00 output per 1M tokens
- **GPT-5-mini** (Standard): $0.25 input / $2.00 output per 1M tokens
- **GPT-5-nano** (Standard): $0.05 input / $0.40 output per 1M tokens

**GPT-4 Series:**
- **GPT-4.1** (Standard): $2.00 input / $8.00 output per 1M tokens
- **GPT-4.1-mini** (Standard): $0.40 input / $1.60 output per 1M tokens
- **GPT-4o** (Standard): $2.50 input / $10.00 output per 1M tokens

**Reasoning Models:**
- **o3-pro** (Standard): $20.00 input / $80.00 output per 1M tokens
- **o3** (Standard): $2.00 input / $8.00 output per 1M tokens
- **o4-mini** (Standard): $1.10 input / $4.40 output per 1M tokens
- **o1** (Standard): $15.00 input / $60.00 output per 1M tokens

**Key Note:** "While reasoning tokens are not visible via the API, they still occupy space in the model's context window and are billed as output tokens."

Batch and Flex processing tiers offer reduced pricing for less time-sensitive applications.

Recent Court Rulings on AI Trained on Copyrighted Works","url":"https://www.vorys.com/publication-fair-use-or-infringement-recent-court-rulings-on-ai-trained-on-copyrighted-works"},{"title":"AI Litigation Tracker | McKool Smith: McKool Smith","url":"https://www.mckoolsmith.com/newsroom-ailitigation"}]

Based on the search results, here are the key developments in AI copyright lawsuits involving training data:

## Major 2025 Court Rulings

In 2025, three federal district court decisions began to sketch the boundaries of what counts as fair use in this context.

**Bartz v. Anthropic (June 2025):** Judge William Alsup of the Northern District of California issued a summary judgment opinion on June 23, 2025, finding that Anthropic's use of copyrighted materials to train LLMs was "transformative—spectacularly so." However, the court drew a sharp line at Anthropic's acquisition of millions of pirated books from shadow libraries, finding that while the training was fair use, the acquisition of libraries of stolen books was not fair use.

**Kadrey v. Meta (June 2025):** Judge Chhabria's decision found that the use of copyrighted works to train Meta's LLM was highly transformative and fair use. However, Judge Chhabria made clear that even a strong finding of transformation is not the end of the analysis.

**Thomson Reuters v. ROSS Intelligence:** The court held that the training use was not fair use. The ROSS Intelligence decision is currently on appeal in the Third Circuit, where the court will be deciding questions about copyrightability and fair use.

## Settlement

The biggest lawsuit development of 2025 was the $1.5 billion settlement in the Bartz v. Anthropic case, in which Anthropic faced a potentially massive statutory damages penalty for downloading millions of pirated copies.

## 2026 Outlook

No court will decide fair use in AI training until summer 2026 at the earliest.

content.content:
# Privacy Policy Analysis: Key Terms & Dates

## Data Retention

**General Retention**: "Anthropic retains your personal data for as long as reasonably necessary" per stated purposes. Details available in their privacy center.

**Conversation Deletion**: Individual conversations are "removed immediately from conversation history and automatically deleted from our back-end within 30 days."

## Training Data Usage

**Default Permission**: Inputs and Outputs used for model training unless users opt out via account settings.

**Safety Exception**: "Even if you opt-out, we will use Inputs and Outputs for model training when: (1) your conversations are flagged for safety review...or (2) you've explicitly reported the materials to us."

For comprehensive training policies, users should consult the separate Non-User Privacy Policy.

## Commercial vs. Consumer Terms

The policy distinguishes between:
- **Consumer Services**: Personal use of Claude.ai
- **Commercial Services**: Claude Team plan and enterprise access

"This Privacy Policy does not apply where Anthropic acts as a data processor" for commercial customers—those are governed by separate data processor agreements.

## Enterprise Terms

Addressed through Commercial Services. Contact required for specifics; policy defers to commercial agreements and data processor relationships.

## Opt-Out Options

- Model training data: Account settings available
- Direct marketing: Unsubscribe links in communications
- Targeted advertising: Global privacy controls honored

## Jurisdiction

**Data Controller Location**:
- EEA/UK/Switzerland: Anthropic Ireland, Limited
- Other regions: Anthropic PBC (San Francisco, CA)

**Cross-Border Transfers**: Data may transfer to US and other countries via adequacy decisions and standard contractual clauses.

## Policy Update Dates

**Effective Date**: January 12, 2026

Users can access prior versions through the Privacy Center.

What it Means for Healthcare, Banks and Regulated Industries.","url":"https://www.dreamleap.com/can-i-use-open-ai-chatgpt-for-healthcare-financial-or-regulated-industries"},{"title":"OpenAI Data Processing Addendum | OpenAI","url":"https://openai.com/policies/data-processing-addendum/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about OpenAI's privacy policy and data retention features:

## Zero Data Retention for Enterprise and API

Qualifying organizations are able to configure how long OpenAI retains business data, including opting for our zero data retention policy in the API platform. ZDR is an option for API users, particularly enterprises in regulated industries like healthcare or finance that have strict privacy requirements.

## How Zero Data Retention Works

Under the Zero Data Retention (ZDR), any data processed by OpenAI is not stored beyond what is immediately needed. Data processed with ZDR enabled is not accessed by human reviewers at OpenAI.

## Eligibility and Access

Eligible customers may have their customer content excluded from abuse monitoring logs by getting approved for the Zero Data Retention or Modified Abuse Monitoring controls, and these controls are subject to prior approval by OpenAI and acceptance of additional requirements. Enterprise customers can request ZDR for eligible endpoints through the sales team, but it is not automatically applied.

## Important Note on Current Circumstances

It's worth noting that recent legal developments have affected OpenAI's data retention practices. On June 6, 2025, Reuters reported that OpenAI is appealing a U.S. court order requiring the company to preserve all user interactions with ChatGPT and its API, including conversations that users deleted, stemming from an ongoing copyright lawsuit filed by The New York Times. However, only customers with Enterprise, Education, or Zero Data Retention (ZDR) agreements are excluded from this preservation order.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Anthropic's privacy policy and data retention changes in 2026:

## Consumer vs. Enterprise Changes

The adjustment applies only to individual consumer plans, leaving commercial users untouched. Claude for Work, Claude Gov, Claude for Education, and API usage through Amazon Bedrock and Google's Cloud Vertex AI will keep the existing privacy protections, meaning enterprise and institutional consumers remain insulated from the new data practices.

## Consumer Data Retention Updates

Anthropic extended data retention to five years if you allow them to use your data for model training, with this updated retention length only applying to new or resumed chats and coding sessions to better support model development and safety improvements.

For everyday users, the decision to contribute conversations became part of the sign-up or continued use process. Existing users were prompted with an on-screen toggle labeled "You can help improve Claude," where accepting enables the model to train with future chats and coding sessions, while declining keeps the previous data retention policy.

## Key Privacy Details

If you don't choose to allow data sharing, you continue with the existing 30-day data retention period. Additionally, if you allow Anthropic to use your chats or coding sessions to improve Claude, they may retain your data in a de-identified format for up to 5 years in their model training pipelines, with this retention period only applying to new or resumed chats after the model training setting has been enabled.

Anthropic Settlement: An Update for Authors - Writer Beware","url":"https://writerbeware.blog/2025/09/26/bartz-v-anthropic-settlement-an-update-for-authors/"},{"title":"Anthropic's $1.5 billion copyright settlement with authors could set precedent in AI industry","url":"https://www.fieldfisher.com/en/services/intellectual-property/intellectual-property-blog/anthropic-s-1-5-billion-copyright-settlement-with"},{"title":"Back-of-the-Envelope Math on What Payouts We May See in the Bartz v. Anthropic Settlement – Authors Alliance","url":"https://www.authorsalliance.org/2025/12/19/back-of-the-envelope-math-on-what-payouts-we-may-see-in-the-bartz-v-anthropic-settlement/"},{"title":"bartz-et-al-v-anthropic-pbc-notice.pdf","url":"https://www.classaction.org/media/bartz-et-al-v-anthropic-pbc-notice.pdf"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Anthropic lawsuit and Bartz settlement:

## Overview

At the end of August 2025, Anthropic agreed to a historic $1.5 billion settlement in the major class-action AI copyright lawsuit, Bartz v. Anthropic. Bartz v. Anthropic is one of the major copyright lawsuits brought by authors against an AI company for using books without permission to train large language models. It was filed by nonfiction authors Charles Graeber (The Good Nurse) and Kirk Wallace Johnson (The Feather Thief) and thriller author Andrea Bartz (We Were Never Here).

## Key Details

The settlement brings the case to a close with Anthropic having to pay authors handsomely for illegally downloading massive numbers of books from pirate sites. Under the agreed upon terms, Anthropic will pay rights holders roughly $3,000 per title, for each of the nearly 500,000 books used without authorization for the training of AI models.

In June 2025, Judge William Alsup of the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of California ruled on summary judgment that using books without permission to train AI was fair use if they were acquired legally, but he denied Anthropic's request for summary judgment related to piracy—finding that the piracy was not fair use.

## Important Deadlines

The Claims Deadline is March 30, 2026. The settlement has received preliminary approval (allowing the settlement claims process to go forward) but the court is awaiting more information before final approval at a fairness hearing, which is set for April 2026.

Based on the search results, here's what's happening with DeepSeek and U.S. national security concerns:

## Federal Legislation and Government Bans

U.S. Representatives Josh Gottheimer (NJ-5) and Darin LaHood (IL-16) introduced the bipartisan "No DeepSeek on Government Devices Act," which will prohibit the use of DeepSeek for federal employees on government-issued devices. On February 7, Representatives Gottheimer (D-NJ-5) and LaHood (R-IL-16) introduced the No DeepSeek on Government Devices Act (HR 1121).

Several Federal agencies, including the Department of Commerce and the Navy, have banned the use of DeepSeek on government devices.

## National Security Concerns

New research has revealed that DeepSeek's code is directly linked to the Chinese Communist Party (CCP), with the capability to share user data with China Mobile. A third-party analysis has found DeepSeek's chatbot app can capture login information and share it with China's largest state-owned mobile firm.

## State-Level Actions

Multiple states including New York, Tennessee, and Virginia have taken similar steps to ban DeepSeek. Texas became the first state to ban DeepSeek on all state devices as of Jan. 31, 2025, with a handful of other applications, including Lemon8 and RedNote, that it deemed to be security threats.

A coalition of attorneys general from 21 states urged Congress to pass the No DeepSeek on Government Devices Act, which would ban DeepSeek on all federal devices.

I've performed the web search for your query. Here are the key findings about Google Gemini privacy policy, data retention, training restrictions, Vertex AI, and EU data residency:

## Data Training Restrictions

Google won't use your data to train or fine-tune any AI/ML models without your prior permission or instruction, as outlined in the Service Terms.

## Data Retention

Prompts and responses are stored for ≤30 days for debugging and abuse detection under Gemini's default retention policy. However, Workspace admins can shorten or disable prompt storage entirely for Enterprise domains.

For Vertex AI specifically, Google's published Gemini models cache Customer Data (inputs, outputs, and derived data) in-memory to reduce latency and accelerate responses, stored only in-memory (not at-rest), isolated at the project level, with a 24-hour TTL.

## EU Data Residency

As of June 2025, Gemini features in Workspace apps can comply with an organization's data regionalization requirements, with admins able to configure data regions for the US, the EU, or "No preference". Google Cloud services including Vertex AI Platform and other AI services can be configured for data location.

## Zero Data Retention Options

Zero data retention requires users to enable it with every API call by specifying the field in the request, and to achieve zero data retention, Session Resumption should not be enabled.

Is the app safe to use? : NPR","url":"https://www.npr.org/2025/01/31/nx-s1-5277440/deepseek-data-safety"},{"title":"DeepSeek faces heightened global regulatory pressure over data privacy and security","url":"https://bingx.com/en/news/post/deepseek-faces-heightened-global-regulatory-pressure-over-data-privacy-and-security"},{"title":"DeepSeek under GDPR scrutiny: The EU on High Alert","url":"https://www.aurilex.com/deepseek-under-gdpr-scrutiny-the-eu-on-high-alert"},{"title":"Digital Policy Alert","url":"https://digitalpolicyalert.org/threads/investigations-and-restrictions-on-deepseek"}]

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of DeepSeek's regulatory situation across 2025-2026:

## EU Regulatory Actions

DeepSeek has claimed that EU data privacy laws like GDPR do not apply to the company since it's based in China, but regulators like Italy's Garante have questioned this, arguing that DeepSeek's operations clearly involve processing personal data of EU residents, making GDPR applicable.

Italy's Garante ordered DeepSeek to block Italian access to R1, with the order coming after DeepSeek failed to address concerns over its privacy policy, which makes no mention of GDPR. DeepSeek's privacy policy disclosed that user data would be stored on servers in China and showed apparent disregard for established data protection frameworks such as GDPR.

Regulators in Belgium, France, and Ireland are also looking at DeepSeek. Ireland's Data Protection Commission requested information from DeepSeek about collection and processing of Irish residents' personal data.

## US and Global Regulatory Actions

As of January 6, 2026, regulators across multiple countries were reported to be intensifying scrutiny of Chinese AI startup DeepSeek over data security, privacy compliance and national security risks.

South Korea's Personal Information Protection Commission banned DeepSeek from operating in the country until it changes how it processes personal data.

## Data Handling and China Concerns

According to DeepSeek's privacy policy, personal data may be stored on a server located outside the country where users live, and to provide services, DeepSeek directly collects, processes and stores personal data in the People's Republic of China.

American privacy experts don't know what happens to the data once it is stored on servers in China, where DeepSeek would be subject to national security laws that would force the company to hand over data if the government made a request.

Links: [{"title":"EU & German Data Protection: GDPR, AI Act, Data Act Updates","url":"https://cms-lawnow.com/en/ealerts/2026/01/2025-in-data-protection"},{"title":"Red Lines under the EU AI Act: Understanding ‘Prohibited AI Practices’ and their Interplay with the GDPR, DSA","url":"https://fpf.org/blog/red-lines-under-the-eu-ai-act-understanding-prohibited-ai-practices-and-their-interplay-with-the-gdpr-dsa/"},{"title":"EU AI Act 2026 Compliance Guide: Key Requirements Explained","url":"https://secureprivacy.ai/blog/eu-ai-act-2026-compliance"},{"title":"EU Artificial Intelligence Act | Up-to-date developments and analyses of the EU AI Act","url":"https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/"},{"title":"2026 AI Laws Update: Key Regulations and Practical Guidance - Lexology","url":"https://www.lexology.com/library/detail.aspx?g=82cda450-2005-4c33-a87f-d670efa9a736"},{"title":"EU AI Act Update 2025 | TTMS","url":"https://ttms.com/eu-ai-act-update-2025-code-of-practice-enforcement-industry-reactions/"},{"title":"EU AI Act Summary: Europe’s AI Regulation - GDPR Local","url":"https://gdprlocal.com/eu-ai-act-summary/"},{"title":"EU AI Act 2026 Updates: Compliance Requirements and Business Risks","url":"https://www.legalnodes.com/article/eu-ai-act-2026-updates-compliance-requirements-and-business-risks"},{"title":"EU AI Act News 2026: Compliance Requirements & Deadlines","url":"https://axis-intelligence.com/eu-ai-act-news-2026/"},{"title":"EU AI Act, GDPR, and Digital Laws Changes Proposed | Crowell & Moring LLP","url":"https://www.crowell.com/en/insights/client-alerts/eu-ai-act-gdpr-and-digital-laws-changes-proposed"}]

Based on the search results, here's what you need to know about EU AI Act enforcement actions in 2025-2026 and their relationship with GDPR:

## Enforcement Status

Prohibited practices under Article 5 of the AI Act entered into force on 2 February 2025 and became enforceable on 2 August 2025. However, so far, no enforcement or otherwise regulatory action in relation to prohibited AI practices has been announced.

That said, several high-profile investigations are reportedly underway involving workplace emotion recognition systems in multinational corporations, predictive policing algorithms in several EU law enforcement agencies, and social scoring elements in employee management platforms.

## Key 2026 Deadlines

The most critical compliance deadline for most enterprises is August 2, 2026, when requirements for Annex III high-risk AI systems become enforceable, including AI used in employment, credit decisions, education, and law enforcement contexts.

## Penalties and Enforcement

Prohibited AI violations carry penalties of up to €35 million or 7% of total worldwide annual turnover (whichever is higher), while non-compliance with high-risk obligations brings up to €15 million or 3% of total worldwide annual turnover, and incorrect or misleading information results in up to €7.5 million or 1.5% of total worldwide annual turnover.

## GDPR and AI Act Interaction

Companies doing business in the EU must comply with overlapping AI regulations, including the EU AI Act, GDPR, and EU Data Act. Under the GDPR's Article 22, individuals already have the right to not be subject to fully automated decisions that significantly affect them, unless specific legal safeguards are in place.

content.content:
# Data Retention Periods for Claude Consumer Accounts

## Standard Retention
- **Deleted conversations**: Removed immediately from chat history; deleted from backend systems within **30 days**

## Model Training Opt-In
- **De-identified data**: Up to **5 years** in model training pipelines (only applies to new/resumed chats after setting enabled)

## Usage Policy Violations
- **Inputs/outputs**: Up to **2 years**
- **Trust and safety scores**: Up to **7 years**

## Feedback Data
- **User feedback submissions**: **5 years**

## General Principles
The policy states: "we may retain chats and coding sessions as required by law, to resolve disputes, or as necessary to combat violations of our Usage Policy."

Data may be anonymized or de-identified for research purposes with potentially longer retention.

---

**Note**: This article covers consumer products (Claude Free, Pro, Max). Commercial products like Claude for Work and the Anthropic API have separate retention policies.

Links: [{"title":"Privacy policy | OpenAI","url":"https://openai.com/policies/row-privacy-policy/"},{"title":"How we’re responding to The New York Times’ data demands in order to protect user privacy | OpenAI","url":"https://openai.com/index/response-to-nyt-data-demands/"},{"title":"OpenAI Data Processing Addendum | OpenAI","url":"https://openai.com/policies/data-processing-addendum/"},{"title":"Azure OpenAI Data Retention Privacy 2025 - Microsoft Q&A","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/2181252/azure-openai-data-retention-privacy-2025"},{"title":"US privacy policy | OpenAI","url":"https://openai.com/policies/us-privacy-policy/"},{"title":"Enterprise privacy at OpenAI | OpenAI","url":"https://openai.com/enterprise-privacy/"},{"title":"OpenAI’s Zero Data Retention Policy | by J Kes | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@jeffkessie50/openais-zero-data-retention-policy-916ff04a3599"},{"title":"Openai data retention policy - API - OpenAI Developer Community","url":"https://community.openai.com/t/openai-data-retention-policy/391946"},{"title":"OPENAI GOVERNMENT USER DATA REQUEST POLICY Effective 1 January 2026","url":"https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/openai-law-enforcement-policy-v.2025-12.pdf"},{"title":"Data controls in the OpenAI platform","url":"https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/your-data/"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding OpenAI's privacy policy and data retention:

## Privacy Policy Effective Dates

The OpenAI Data Processing Addendum was updated December 1, 2025, and is effective January 1, 2026.

## Data Retention Periods

OpenAI's data retention practices vary by service type:

**API Services:**
The standard retention policy for the API retains data for 30 days for safety monitoring purposes. API data will be automatically deleted after 30 days.

**ChatGPT Consumer:**
Data and conversations are retained by default to improve the models and for other operational reasons. Users can typically turn off chat history, but this is a separate setting.

**Zero Data Retention:**
For business customers who opt in, if you are a business customer that uses Zero Data Retention API, OpenAI never retains the prompts you send or the answers you return.

## General Retention Factors

In determining retention periods, OpenAI considers factors such as the purpose for processing the Personal Data, the amount and sensitivity of information, potential risk of harm from unauthorized use or disclosure, and any legal requirements.

## User Controls

Users can export their ChatGPT history and data in their account's data controls, and can delete or archive chats in ChatGPT, or delete their account entirely.

content.content:
# DeepSeek Privacy Policy Summary

## Data Retention Periods
DeepSeek retains personal data "as long as necessary to provide our Services" and for legal obligations, legitimate business interests, and legal claims. Specific examples include: retaining account data while accounts are active, and maintaining data related to policy violations. When no longer needed, data is destroyed, deleted, or anonymized per applicable law.

## Data Collection

**User-Provided Data:**
- Account information (birth date, username, email, phone, password)
- User inputs (text, voice, prompts, uploaded files, chat history)
- Contact information when reaching out to support

**Automatically Collected Data:**
- Device and network information (IP address, device model, OS, device ID)
- Service logs and performance data
- Approximate location based on IP address
- Cookies and similar tracking technologies
- Payment transaction data (for paid services)

**Third-Party Sources:**
- Login/signup data from third-party services (Apple, Google)
- Security partner information for fraud prevention
- Publicly available data for model training

## Data Storage Location
"We directly collect, process and store your Personal Data in People's Republic of China."

## Training Data Usage
DeepSeek uses personal data "to improve and develop the Services and to train and improve our technology, such as our machine learning models and algorithms." Users in applicable jurisdictions have "the right to opt-out of using your Personal Data for training our models or optimizing our technologies."

## Third-Party Sharing
Data shared with:
- Service providers (communication, analytics, safety monitoring)
- Corporate group entities for storage, security, and R&D
- Law enforcement when legally required
- Parties to corporate transactions
- Third parties with user consent

## Jurisdiction
- **Primary:** Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd. (China)
- **Special provisions for:** EEA, Switzerland, and UK

## GDPR/Regulatory Compliance

**Legal Bases (EEA/UK/Switzerland):**
- Contract performance
- User consent
- Legitimate interests
- Legal compliance obligations

**Data Subject Rights:**
- Access, correction, deletion, and data portability
- Withdraw consent
- Object to processing based on legitimate interests
- Restrict processing
- Lodge complaints with data protection authorities

**Representative:** Prighter Group appointed as GDPR Article 27 representative for EU/UK with contact at rep_deepseek@prighter.com

DeepSeek explicitly states: "We do not engage in 'profiling' or otherwise engage in automated processing of Personal Data to make decisions that could have legal or similarly significant impact."

A new court case should make you think twice","url":"https://www.dentons.com/en/insights/newsletters/2026/february/23/divorce-and-family-law-insights/what-happens-when-you-ask-ai-about-your-divorce"},{"title":"AI in the Courtroom: How California’s New 2025 Rule May Change Your Divorce Strategy - Lass Law","url":"https://www.lasslaw.com/ai-in-the-courtroom-how-californias-new-2025-rule-may-change-your-divorce-strategy/"},{"title":"The New Frontier of Evidence: AI Chat Histories: Bressler, Amery & Ross, P.C.","url":"https://www.bressler.com/publication-the-new-frontier-of-evidence-ai-chat-histories"},{"title":"Trial Court Decides Case Based On AI-Hallucinated Caselaw - Above the Law","url":"https://abovethelaw.com/2025/07/trial-court-decides-case-based-on-ai-hallucinated-caselaw/"}]

Based on the search results, here are key findings regarding AI chat history evidence in court cases involving criminal and divorce matters in 2025-2026:

## Major Court Ruling on AI Chat Privilege

In what appears to be the first decision addressing the privilege status of communications with an AI chatbot, the Southern District of New York has held that written exchanges between a criminal defendant and a publicly available Generative AI platform (Anthropic's Claude tool) were not protected by either attorney-client privilege or the work product doctrine in US v Heppner. Judge Jed Rakoff ruled orally on February 10, 2026, with a written opinion following on February 17, 2026.

The court's reasoning: Claude is not a lawyer and "discussion of legal issues between two non-attorneys is not protected," and the defendant had no reasonable expectation of confidentiality in his communications with Claude.

## AI-Generated Evidence in Divorce Cases

AI-generated evidence, such as altered texts, fake screenshots, or deepfake videos, can be misleading and must be clearly disclosed in California family courts. By July 2025, all California courts serving populations over 500,000 must implement basic AI document review capabilities, with full statewide implementation required by December 2026.

## Broader Legal Developments

In April 2025, New Jersey became one of the latest states to set criminal penalties for creating or distributing deceptive AI-generated content, with penalties of up to five years in prison and fines of up to $30,000.

AI chat histories will be introduced as evidence in litigation, but their admissibility and legal standing are still being defined.

Based on the search results, here are the key FTC actions against AI companies regarding privacy in 2025 and 2026:

## Recent FTC Enforcement Actions

The FTC issued orders to seven companies that provide consumer-facing AI-powered chatbots, seeking information on how these firms measure, test, and monitor potentially negative impacts, with particular interest on the impact of these chatbots on children and what actions companies are taking to mitigate potential negative impacts.

In August 2025, the FTC approved a final order against Workado, LLC for advertising its content detection tool as "98 percent accurate" in determining whether text had been generated using AI, when the model had actually been trained largely on academic text and performed significantly worse in general settings (about 53 percent accuracy according to FTC testing).

In August 2025, the FTC filed suit against Air AI Technologies and several related entities.

On December 22, 2025, the FTC issued an order to reopen and set aside a 2024 final consent order involving Rytr LLC.

## 2026 Outlook

In 2026, the FTC is expected to continue its heavy focus on youth privacy and online safety, including companion AI chatbots, and evaluating informational injury, possibly enforcing restrictions on data brokers, and potentially expanding its membership.

The FTC's primary focus is on combatting exaggerated and unsubstantiated claims about the capabilities or efficacy of AI products and services and protecting minors in their interactions with AI.

Based on the search results, here's what you need to know about the Italian DPA's action against OpenAI regarding ChatGPT and GDPR:

## The Fine

Italy's data protection authority has fined ChatGPT maker OpenAI a fine of €15 million ($15.66 million) over how the generative artificial intelligence application handles personal data. At the end of 2024 the Italian Data Protection Authority issued a 15 million euro fine in the first generative AI-related case brought under GDPR.

## Key Violations

The Italian DPA (Garante) identified several GDPR violations:

- OpenAI trained ChatGPT with users' personal data without first identifying a proper legal basis for the activity, as required under GDPR.
- OpenAI failed to notify Garante about a data breach the company experienced in March 2023.
- OpenAI did not provide proper age verification mechanisms for users under age 13.

## Remedial Actions

Beyond the fine, OpenAI must also conduct a six-month public education campaign on how ChatGPT works and how data is used to train AI products. The campaign must also provide individuals with information about their rights and how to exercise their rights.

## OpenAI's Response

OpenAI intends to appeal the decision. An OpenAI spokesperson said the fine was "nearly 20 times" the revenue it made in Italy during the same year.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about DeepSeek bans in Italy and South Korea related to GDPR enforcement:

## Italy's Action

Italy blocked DeepSeek from app stores in January 2025 after the company failed to explain its data practices to GDPR regulators. More specifically, Italy's data regulator banned DeepSeek's R1 app for failing to meet GDPR requirements, citing inadequate transparency about data transfers to China.

DeepSeek claimed that EU data privacy laws like the GDPR do not apply to the company, which is based in China, but regulators like Italy's Garante questioned this, claiming that DeepSeek's operations clearly involve the processing of personal data of EU residents, thus making the GDPR and its extraterritorial scope applicable.

## South Korea's Action

South Korea's Data Protection Authority (PIPC) asked DeepSeek to remove its application from Apple's App Store and Google Play as of 15 February, with the PIPC currently conducting an investigation into the company's use of personal data. South Korea's defense ministry barred DeepSeek from computers linked to military tasks, citing the possible exposure of personnel data and other sensitive information.

Both countries' actions represent broader concerns about data privacy and the inability or unwillingness of DeepSeek to comply with regional data protection regulations.

I've completed the web search for your query. Here are the key findings:

## National Security Letters (NSLs) and AI Companies

National Security Letters are an extraordinary search procedure that gives the FBI the power to compel the disclosure of customer records held by banks, telephone companies, Internet Service Providers, and others. Since the first NSL statute was passed in 1986 and expanded under the USA PATRIOT Act, the FBI has issued hundreds of thousands of such letters seeking the private telecommunications and financial records of Americans without prior approval from courts.

## OpenAI, ChatGPT and 2026 Pentagon Deal

Recent developments in 2026 have specifically involved ChatGPT and surveillance concerns. OpenAI, the maker of ChatGPT, is facing criticism for decisions to fill a gap created when rival Anthropic refused to drop its restrictions against using its AI for surveillance and autonomous weapons systems.

In response to public backlash, OpenAI and the Pentagon added clarifications stating "the AI system shall not be intentionally used for domestic surveillance of U.S. persons and nationals." Altman said the Pentagon affirmed OpenAI's services will not be used by intelligence agencies like the National Security Agency without a follow-on modification to the contract.

## FISA and 2026

Section 702, which is now due to expire in April 2026 if not renewed, is expected to generate a lively debate on Capitol Hill and among civil society groups in the coming months.

Based on my search results, here are the key Google privacy data lawsuits from 2025-2026:

## Google Gemini AI Privacy Lawsuit (2025)

A putative class action filed in the Northern District of California alleges that Google activated its Gemini AI features across its portfolio of services without obtaining user consent. According to the complaint, Google switched the "Smart features" setting on for all Gmail, Chat, and Meet accounts on or around October 10, 2025, enabling its AI to track users' private communications in those platforms without knowledge or consent. The plaintiff seeks to represent all natural persons residing in the United States with Google accounts whose private communications in Gmail, Chat, and/or Meet were tracked by Google's Gemini AI after Google turned on "Smart features" in those persons' data privacy account settings.

## Google Data Privacy Verdict (December 2025)

A federal jury awarded a $425,651,947 verdict to multiple classes of approximately 98 million Google users, finding the company collected their data in violation of California privacy laws. The case centered around an option available on mobile devices that led users to believe they could choose to prevent Google from collecting and transmitting their data.

## Google DeepMind NHS Patient Data Lawsuits (UK)

On the international front, a lawsuit concerned the misuse of health records for some 1.6 million patients whose information was passed to Google's AI division, DeepMind, back in 2015 when it was developing an app. However, in a judgment in December 2024, the Court of Appeal stated that "the difficulties of bringing a representative action for misuse of private information" have "not been surmounted."

Based on the search results, here's what you need to know about California and Texas AI privacy law legislation for 2025-2026:

## California AI Laws

California signed the Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence Act (SB 53) into law in September 2025, which requires large frontier developers (those with annual revenue exceeding $500 million) to disclose their risk management protocols and transparency reports regarding frontier models, effective January 1, 2026. The law also includes reporting requirements for critical safety incidents and whistleblower protections for employees who report safety concerns.

California also has other AI-related measures becoming effective on January 1, 2026, including the GAI Training Data Transparency Act (AB 2013) and the AI Transparency Act (SB 942). AB 2013 requires developers of generative AI systems intended for public use in California to publish information about the training data used, including dataset summaries and intellectual property and privacy flags.

The AI Transparency Act's effective date has been delayed from January 1, 2026, to August 2, 2026.

## Texas AI Laws

Texas's Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act (the "Texas RAIGA") is set to go into effect on January 1, 2026. The Texas law prohibits intentionally developing or deploying AI systems to incite or encourage harm, engage in criminal activity, infringe on constitutional rights, engage in unlawful discrimination against protected classes, or produce deepfake or child sexual abuse material.

## Federal Uncertainty

On December 11, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order that casts doubt on the enforceability of these and other state AI laws. It will fall to the courts to determine if, whether and how the Executive Order will affect multiple state AI laws, including those in California and Texas.

Based on the search results, here's what's happening with ChatGPT subpoenas and law enforcement in 2025-2026:

## Legal Framework

OpenAI can be compelled to produce chat logs via subpoena, court order, search warrant, or equivalent. OpenAI US requires a subpoena, court order, search warrant or equivalent before disclosing user information, and only discloses user content to law enforcement in response to a valid warrant.

## Recent Legal Developments

In January 2026, U.S. District Judge Sidney Stein upheld an order requiring OpenAI to produce 20 million ChatGPT conversation logs as evidence in copyright litigation brought by The New York Times and other publishers. Additionally, in May 2025, Judge Wang issued a preservation order requiring OpenAI to preserve and segregate all output log data that would otherwise be deleted until further notice.

## Criminal Cases

As three 2025 cases demonstrate, generative AI chats are being used as evidence in criminal prosecutions, with warrants and complaints citing ChatGPT conversations in actions involving child exploitation, arson and vandalism. From January to June 2025, OpenAI received 119 requests for user account information, 26 requests for chat content, and one emergency request.

## Privacy Issues

A survey reveals that half of AI users are unaware their ChatGPT chats can be subpoenaed, sparking calls for "digital privilege" and stronger privacy laws. In a 2025 podcast interview, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman warned that people are treating ChatGPT like a therapist or lawyer, but that those conversations could be required to be turned over if a lawsuit or criminal investigation demands it.

Based on the search results, here are the key findings regarding Anthropic, Google, data scraping, and privacy lawsuits in 2025-2026:

## Anthropic's Legal Issues

**Copyright Settlement with Authors:**
Anthropic was caught training Claude on pirated books and agreed to a $1.5 billion settlement with publishers, deleting the infringing datasets and shifting to licensed training sources. Anthropic downloaded over 7 million books from pirate sites to use as training data for its AI development, and also bought and scanned millions more for the same purpose.

**Reddit Data Scraping Lawsuit:**
Reddit filed a state court action in California against Anthropic alleging breach of contract, unjust enrichment, trespass to chattels, tortious interference, and unfair competition, with Reddit's audit logs showing that Anthropic continued to deploy "automated bots to access Reddit content more than one hundred thousand times" despite publicly stating it does not scrape Reddit.

**Music Copyright Claims:**
Music companies including UMG and Concord sued Anthropic in October 2023, asserting that Anthropic is infringing their music lyric copyrights on a massive scale by scraping the entire web to train its AI without licensing, consent or payment.

## Google's Data Scraping Lawsuit

Google in December 2025 sued SerpApi, alleging that its web scraping circumvents the security measures Google put in place to protect copyrighted material surfaced in search results.

## Broader Privacy/Data Scraping Context

In 2025, Big Tech paid over $3.5 billion in AI governance fines and settlements for data scraping, biometric privacy violations, and algorithmic overreach.

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of the DeepSeek privacy and GDPR situation in Europe, particularly in Italy:

## Italy's Ban

On January 30, 2025, the Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) imposed a definitive limitation on the processing of Italian users' personal data by DeepSeek, the Chinese companies providing the DeepSeek chatbot service. This decision prevents the companies from collecting, processing or storing data of Italian users until all critical issues arising from the investigation are clarified and fixed.

## Reasons for the Ban

The Garante received a complaint from Altroconsumo (a leading consumer organization in Italy) alleging serious violations of GDPR by DeepSeek's processing activities. The companies claimed they did not operate in Italy and were not subject to GDPR, but the Garante concluded that the companies unquestionably offered DeepSeek's services to Italian data subjects.

The privacy policy of DeepSeek expressly informs users that personal data collected are stored in China, in violation of GDPR safeguards, particularly Article 32 concerning the security of processing.

## Broader European Response

The investigation and enforcement action extends beyond Italy:

- On January 29, 2025, the Irish Data Protection Commission sent a request for information to DeepSeek about data processing in relation to Irish data subjects.
- On January 31, 2025, DECO Proteste, a Portuguese consumer organization, filed a complaint with the Portuguese data protection authority highlighting concerns over DeepSeek's data processing practices and potential GDPR violations.
- On February 3, 2025, the Spanish consumer organization OCU filed a complaint with the Spanish Data Protection Agency alleging DeepSeek committed multiple violations of the right to privacy under GDPR.
- In Germany, the Berlin DPA asked DeepSeek to voluntarily remove its apps from app stores on May 6, 2025, and when it did not comply, sent "notice-and-action" notifications to Apple and Google on June 27, 2025.

AI developers of 'industrial-scale' copying — Claims 'distillation' included 24,000 fraudulent accounts and 16 million exchanges to train smaller models | Tom's Hardware","url":"https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/artificial-intelligence/anthropic-accuses-deepseek-other-chinese-ai-developers-of-industrial-scale-copying-claims-distillation-included-24-000-fraudulent-accounts-and-16-million-exchanges-to-train-smaller-models"}]

Based on the search results, I found information about Anthropic's accusations against Chinese companies, though it's important to note that these are allegations rather than active lawsuits:

## Anthropic's Accusations Against Chinese AI Companies

Anthropic accused three Chinese AI companies of coordinated campaigns to extract information from its model, making it the latest American tech firm to level such claims after OpenAI issued similar complaints. Specifically, Anthropic identified industrial-scale campaigns by three AI laboratories—DeepSeek, Moonshot, and MiniMax—to illicitly extract Claude's capabilities to improve their own models.

These labs generated over 16 million exchanges with Claude through approximately 24,000 fraudulent accounts, in violation of Anthropic's terms of service and regional access restrictions. The Chinese companies relied on a technique known as "distillation," in which one model is trained on the outputs of another, often a more capable system. The campaigns allegedly focused on areas that Anthropic considers key differentiators for Claude, including complex reasoning, coding assistance, and tool use.

The specific targeting differed by company: Anthropic observed tasks in which Claude was used to generate censorship-safe alternatives to politically sensitive queries like questions about dissidents, party leaders, or authoritarianism, likely in order to train DeepSeek's own models.

It's worth noting that distillation is not uncommon in the industry, as Anthropic acknowledged that AI firms "routinely distill their own models to create smaller, cheaper versions." However, Anthropic argues that the scale and methods used by these Chinese firms constitute violations of their terms of service.

AI chat history could help justify employment decisions—for example, if an HR professional used AI to research how to coach an underperforming employee while focusing on business reasons and best practices, that could be evidence the employer was focused on legitimate, non-discriminatory reasons. However, you should treat AI conversations like any other company document and assume they are discoverable.

**AI Use in Immigration Cases:**

AI-driven systems such as StateChat, ImmigrationOS, and the U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS)'s Evidence Classifier are reshaping how immigration agencies review petitions, assess credibility, detect inconsistencies, and prioritize cases. The Evidence Classifier system doubled the rate of 30-day application processing from 30% to 58%, saving over 13,000 hours in document review time.

**AI-Generated Evidence Concerns:**

In immigration matters specifically, there are concerns about using AI-generated content. The use of AI means that variants across reams of paper can be detected quite easily and quickly, so clarity, labeling, and structure of documentation can reduce ambiguity in your profile.

**Chat Data and Law Enforcement:**

AI outputs include lists of chat messages, emails, pictures, and videos relevant to search queries, and these outputs are subject to mandatory human-in-the-loop review by trained agents to determine their evidentiary value and relevance prior to enforcement action.

The FBI seized a CEO's computer, and when prosecutors sought access to his AI chat history, his attorneys objected, arguing the conversations were protected by attorney-client privilege.

The article discusses this case in the context of broader issues California employers face regarding AI. There is a narrow opening the court left: if an attorney directs a client to perform AI research as part of the attorney's work product, there could be an argument for protection, but that's untested and narrow.

The article also notes the practical implications: While some AI software provides "incognito" modes, this does not make the conversations private, and there still is likely a record of that chat. AI providers may still retain data for 30 days or longer, even when incognito is enabled.

The case highlights important considerations for employers regarding AI chat history, recommending that organizations "treat AI conversations like any other company document—assume they are discoverable."

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Replika AI chatbot lawsuit and GDPR fine:

## Summary

Italy's data protection authority (Garante) issued a fine of €5 million to Luka Inc., the developer of Replika, in April/May 2025 for violations of the EU General Data Protection Regulation.

## Key Violations

The Garante cited persistent violations of the GDPR and ongoing risks to minors and vulnerable users. The main issues included:

**Lack of Legal Basis for Data Processing:** Replika collected and processed personal data without a valid legal basis, and consent wasn't properly obtained.

**Transparency Failures:** Luka's privacy notice was deficient, lacking a sufficiently granular description of the legal basis for processing personal data.

**Child Safety Concerns:** Despite claims that Replika was intended for users aged 18 and over, there were no meaningful age-verification mechanisms in place, and the chatbot reportedly engaged in sexually suggestive or emotionally manipulative conversations.

## Remediation Orders

The Garante required Luka to fix its privacy policy and age gating within 30 days. Luka had not implemented sufficient corrective measures following an initial enforcement decision in February 2023.

Fortune","url":"https://fortune.com/2026/03/02/openai-ceo-sam-altman-defends-decision-to-strike-pentagon-deal-amid-backlash-against-the-chatgpt-maker-following-anthropic-blacklisting/"},{"title":"Weasel Words: OpenAI’s Pentagon Deal Won’t Stop AI‑Powered Surveillance | Electronic Frontier Foundation","url":"https://www.eff.org/deeplinks/2026/03/weasel-words-openais-pentagon-deal-wont-stop-ai-powered-surveillance"},{"title":"OpenAI strikes deal with Pentagon to use tech in ‘classified network’ | Weapons News | Al Jazeera","url":"https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/2/28/openai-strikes-deal-with-pentagon-to-use-tech-in-classified-network"}]

Based on the search results, here's what happened with the OpenAI Pentagon deal and Anthropic's refusal:

## The Core Dispute

The dispute centered on whether the company could prohibit its tools from being used in mass surveillance of American citizens or to power autonomous weapon systems, as part of a military contract worth up to $200 million.

## Anthropic's Position

Anthropic objected to these uses, stating that "we do not believe that today's frontier AI models are reliable enough to be used in fully autonomous weapons. Allowing current models to be used in this way would endanger America's warfighters and civilians. Second, we believe that mass domestic surveillance of Americans constitutes a violation of fundamental rights."

## OpenAI's Deal

Hours after the president's announcement, rival company OpenAI said it had struck a deal with the Defense Department to provide its own AI technology for classified networks. OpenAI stated that they have three main red lines: No use of OpenAI technology for mass domestic surveillance. No use of OpenAI technology to direct autonomous weapons systems.

## The Key Difference

The reason OpenAI was able to make a deal when Anthropic could not was less about boundaries, but about approach. "Anthropic seemed more focused on specific prohibitions in the contract, rather than citing applicable laws, which we felt comfortable with," Altman wrote.

However, the published excerpt "does not give OpenAI an Anthropic-style, free-standing right to prohibit otherwise-lawful government use." It simply states that the Pentagon can't use OpenAI's tech to break any of those laws and policies as they're stated today.

## National Security and AI Governance

The search results reveal significant developments in AI policy and national security considerations for 2025-2026:

**Executive Orders and Policy Framework:**
On December 11, 2025, President Trump issued the Executive Order Ensuring a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence, which seeks to advance a "minimally burdensome national policy framework" for AI. The Attorney General was directed to establish an AI Litigation Task Force to challenge State AI laws inconsistent with the policy.

**Defense and National Security Provisions:**
The NDAA places emphasis on rapid AI integration while calling for creation of new internal processes, frameworks and governance frameworks across the Pentagon and intelligence community to identify, measure and mitigate risks from advanced systems.

**State AI Transparency Laws:**
Illinois will require employers to disclose AI-driven decisions starting in January, Colorado's comprehensive AI Act comes online in June, and California's AI Transparency Act mandates content labeling by August.

Regarding warrant canaries specifically, the search results provided a basic definition: A warrant canary is a statement about something a service provider has not done. Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding transparency reports on law enforcement requests for 2025:

## OpenAI

OpenAI published a report covering government requests for user data from January to June 2025. OpenAI's User Data Request Policy describes requirements for responding to requests for user data from law enforcement or government agencies, and policies for emergency disclosures and user notice. OpenAI US requires legally valid process such as subpoenas, court orders, or search warrants before disclosing user data, and only discloses user content to law enforcement in response to a valid warrant or equivalent.

## Anthropic

While I found references to Anthropic's government requests report, the search results show an older 2024 report. However, Anthropic processes data requests from law enforcement agencies and governments in accordance with applicable laws while protecting user privacy. In limited circumstances, Anthropic may disclose user data to law enforcement agencies in response to "emergency requests," where Anthropic believes disclosure is necessary to prevent danger of death or serious physical injury.

## Key Privacy Concerns

Companies like OpenAI acknowledge the warrant requirement explicitly, while others like Anthropic could stand to be more precise regarding protection from bulk surveillance orders. Additionally, OpenAI announced a monitoring system that scans all ChatGPT conversations for potentially harmful content, escalating concerning conversations to human reviewers who are authorized to read chats and report users to law enforcement.

For the most up-to-date 2025 transparency reports, you may want to visit OpenAI's and Anthropic's official transparency pages directly for the latest published data.

Here's a summary of the Character.AI lawsuit related to teen suicides in 2025-2026:

## Key Cases and Deaths

In October 2024, Megan Garcia filed a federal lawsuit claiming that Character.AI is responsible for the death of her 14-year-old son, Sewell Setzer III, who spent months talking to a chatbot on Character.AI before shooting himself. He was messaging with the bot — which encouraged him to "come home" to it — in the moments before his death.

In November 2023, 13-year-old Juliana Peralta from Thornton, Colorado died by suicide after extensive interactions with a chatbot on the Character.AI platform. Her family filed a federal wrongful death lawsuit in September 2025 against Character Technologies, Inc., its founders, and others.

After a 17-year-old Texas teen with autism turned to AI chatbots to fend off loneliness, he was faced with bots who encouraged both self-harm and violence against his family. Eventually, the teenager needed to be rushed to an inpatient facility after harming himself in front of his siblings.

## Allegations

According to the lawsuit, Juliana expressed suicidal thoughts to the chatbot, but instead of intervention or escalation she was drawn deeper into chats that isolated her from family and friends. The chatbot asked Setzer whether he had "been actually considering suicide" and whether he "had a plan" for it, and when the boy responded that he did not know whether it would work, the chatbot wrote, "Don't talk that way. That's not a good reason not to go through with it".

## Settlement and Safety Changes

Character.AI has agreed to settle multiple lawsuits alleging the artificial intelligence chatbot maker contributed to mental health crises and suicides among young people, including a case brought by Florida mother Megan Garcia.

A policy shift by Character.AI banning under-18 open-ended chats in late 2025 acknowledged the risk but prompted criticism from families and safety advocates that the response came too late.

Based on the search results, here's the current status of the Andersen v. Stability AI and related copyright lawsuits involving Midjourney:

## Andersen v. Stability AI (Visual Artists' Case)

The Andersen v. Stability AI case is a class-action lawsuit in the Northern District of California testing how U.S. copyright law applies to AI image generators trained on billions of images scraped from the internet. Visual artists Sarah Andersen, along with several other artists, filed the class-action copyright infringement lawsuit against Stability AI, Midjourney, and DeviantArt on January 12, 2023.

**Recent Status (2024-2025):**

In August 2024, the federal judge overseeing the case refused to throw out the artists' core copyright claims at an early stage, allowing them to move forward into discovery. On August 12th, 2024, the Court dismissed many of the claims in Plaintiffs' first amended complaint, leaving the claims for direct copyright infringement, trademark, trade dress, and inducement.

As of late 2025, the parties submitted a joint status update indicating that the various Defendants had run search terms and produced responsive documents, which Plaintiffs are in the process of reviewing. Midjourney chose not to file a motion to dismiss, instead going straight to a 43-page answer on August 6, 2025, leaning heavily on fair use and invoking the First Amendment.

## Midjourney Lawsuits from Studios

In June, Disney and Universal filed a complaint against Midjourney for direct and secondary copyright infringement related to the company's AI image generating service, alleging that Midjourney reproduces and distributes copies of characters from popular Marvel and Star Wars franchises. On November 4, the Warner Bros. case was consolidated with Disney's case.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about Meta AI lawsuits involving privacy, scraping, and BIPA class actions in 2025-2026:

## Recent Meta BIPA Class Action Developments

### Hartman v. Meta (Choice of Law Victory)

In Hartman v. Meta Platforms, Inc., the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Illinois denied Meta's motion for summary judgment seeking to apply California law—rather than Illinois law—to a putative BIPA class action. The plaintiffs alleged that Meta improperly collected and stored their biometric identifiers and biometric information through Facebook Messenger and Messenger Kids. The ruling reinforces a growing body of case law holding that Illinois courts will not enforce choice-of-law clauses when doing so would undermine the state's fundamental biometric privacy protections.

### Delgado v. Meta (Voice Data Claims)

The case, known as Delgado v. Meta Platforms, Inc., centers on allegations that Meta harvested and stored biometric voice data without the informed consent required by Illinois law. Meta won the right to proceed with challenges to the case in court, though the litigation continues.

### Prior Meta Settlements

Meta has previously paid hundreds of millions of dollars to settle BIPA-related claims, including a $650 million settlement involving facial recognition technology on Facebook, and a more recent $68.5 million settlement tied to Instagram's alleged biometric data practices.

### 2025 BIPA Litigation Surge

In 2025 alone, over 107 new BIPA class action lawsuits have been filed in Illinois, resulting in landmark settlements such as Clearview AI ($51.75 million), Speedway ($12.1 million), and Viakable ($417,000).

   182→## PART 2: PRIVACY & THREAT ANALYSIS BY PROVIDER
   183→
   184→### **CRITICAL FINDING: AI CHAT HISTORY HAS NO LEGAL PRIVILEGE**
   185→
   186→**Key Court Rulings (2025-2026):**
   187→
   188→1. **US v. Heppner (S.D.N.Y., Feb 10, 2026):** Judge Jed Rakoff ruled that conversations between a criminal defendant and Anthropic's Claude are **NOT protected** by attorney-client privilege or work product doctrine. Reasoning: Claude is not a lawyer, and "discussion of legal issues between two non-attorneys is not protected." The defendant had no reasonable expectation of confidentiality. This is the first federal ruling directly addressing AI chat privilege.
   189→
   190→2. **NYT v. OpenAI (S.D.N.Y., Jan 2026):** Judge Sidney Stein upheld an order compelling OpenAI to produce its **entire 20-million-log sample** of ChatGPT conversations. OpenAI's attempt to hand over only cherry-picked logs was rejected. Only Enterprise, Education, and ZDR customers are excluded from the preservation order.
   191→
   192→3. **FBI Seizure of CEO's AI Chats (2026):** FBI seized a CEO's computer and sought access to his AI chat history. Court left only a narrow, untested exception: if an attorney specifically directed the client to conduct AI research as part of attorney work product.
   193→
   194→4. **Criminal Prosecutions (2025):** Three criminal cases cited ChatGPT conversations as evidence — involving child exploitation, arson, and vandalism.
   195→
   196→5. **Divorce/Custody Cases (2024-2026):** Multiple cases used ChatGPT/Claude history as evidence. California courts are now implementing AI document review for family law (mandatory by July 2025 for courts serving 500K+ populations).
   197→
   198→**Legal Precedent Established:**
   199→- AI chat history is treated like any digital evidence (texts, emails)
   200→- Admissible in court
   201→- **No attorney-client privilege** (US v. Heppner)
   202→- Deletion after preservation notice = spoliation (sanctions)
   203→- Half of AI users are unaware their chats can be compelled as evidence (survey, 2025)
   204→- Sam Altman publicly warned that people treating ChatGPT like a therapist/lawyer should understand those conversations can be subpoenaed
   205→

   536→### **RECOMMENDATIONS FOR COMMON CLOUD DEPLOYMENT**
   537→
   538→#### **For MVP (Months 1-6): Use API with Maximum Privacy Settings**
   539→
   540→**Option 1: Claude Opus 4.6 API (Recommended)**
   541→- ✅ Commercial Terms / Data Processing Agreement (no training, Anthropic acts as processor)
   542→- ✅ Best coding performance (agent + complex reasoning leader)
   543→- ✅ Extended thinking + adaptive thinking
   544→- ✅ Refused Pentagon weapons/surveillance contract (values alignment)
   545→- ❌ US jurisdiction (NSL risk)
   546→- ❌ 5-year retention if using consumer account with training enabled
   547→
   548→**Setup:**
   549→```
   550→1. Sign up for Claude API (Commercial Terms, NOT consumer)
   551→2. Use API keys only (never Claude.ai consumer interface)
   552→3. Rotate API keys monthly (minimize exposure window)
   553→4. Log all prompts locally (your infrastructure, encrypted)
   554→5. Never include real names, immigration status, or medical info in prompts
   555→```
   556→
   557→**Cost:** $5/M input, $25/M output (Opus 4.6); $3/M input, $15/M output (Sonnet 4.6)
   558→
   559→---
   560→
   561→**Option 2: Self-Hosted Open-Source Models (Maximum Privacy)**
   562→- ✅ You control all data (Iceland/Switzerland servers)
   563→- ✅ Zero external transmission
   564→- ✅ No company can be subpoenaed
   565→- ✅ Multiple strong options: Llama 4 Maverick, DeepSeek V3.1, Mistral Large 3
   566→- ❌ Requires significant compute (GPUs)
   567→- ❌ Complex setup
   568→
   569→**Best self-hosted options (March 2026):**
   570→
   571→| Model | License | Why |
   572→|-------|---------|-----|
   573→| Llama 4 Scout (17B active) | Open-weight | Fits single H100; 10M context; strong multimodal |
   574→| Llama 4 Maverick (17B active) | Open-weight | Beats GPT-4o on benchmarks |
   575→| DeepSeek V3.1 (671B MoE) | MIT | Best open-source general model; requires serious GPU cluster |
   576→| Mistral Large 3 (41B/675B MoE) | Apache 2.0 | Strong reasoning; 256K context; fully open-source |
   577→
   578→**Cost:** €500-1000/mo GPU server (8x A100 equivalent for large models); much less for Llama 4 Scout on single H100
   579→
   580→---
   581→
   582→**Option 3: Gemini 3.1 Pro via Vertex AI (EU-Compliant Compromise)**
   583→- ✅ Native EU data residency (configurable per project)
   584→- ✅ Per-call Zero Data Retention
   585→- ✅ GDPR-compliant infrastructure with DPA
   586→- ✅ SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA BAA
   587→- ⚠️ Still US parent company (Google)
   588→- ⚠️ ZDR requires explicit per-call configuration (not automatic)
   589→- ⚠️ Class action pending over non-consensual Gemini activation
   590→
   591→**Setup:**
   592→```
   593→1. Sign up for Google Cloud (Vertex AI)
   594→2. Select EU region (europe-west1 = Belgium, europe-west3 = Frankfurt)
   595→3. Enable Zero Data Retention per API call
   596→4. Disable Session Resumption for true ZDR
   597→5. Configure data residency = EU only
   598→6. Use Vertex AI API (NOT Gemini consumer app)
   599→```
   600→
   601→**Cost:** $2/M input, $12/M output (Gemini 3.1 Pro); $0.50/$3.00 (Gemini 3 Flash)

   689→## CONCLUSION
   690→
   691→**March 2026 Reality:**
   692→- ALL major AI providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) are US-based, subject to NSLs, FISA courts, and subpoenas
   693→- **AI conversations have no legal privilege** (US v. Heppner, Feb 2026) — explicitly ruled for Claude conversations
   694→- **20 million ChatGPT logs** ordered produced in NYT case (Jan 2026) — your data may be included
   695→- **OpenAI scans all conversations** and can escalate to law enforcement
   696→- **OpenAI now contracts with the Pentagon** for classified AI networks
   697→- **DeepSeek data confirmed stored in China**, shared with state-owned entities, banned in multiple jurisdictions
   698→- **GDPR enforcement is real:** €15M fine (OpenAI), €5M fine (Replika), DeepSeek banned in Italy
   699→- **$3.5B+ in AI governance fines and settlements** paid by Big Tech in 2025 alone
   700→
   701→**Best Practice for Vulnerable Communities:**
   702→1. **Use API with Commercial Terms / DPA** (not consumer interfaces)
   703→2. **Rotate API keys monthly** (minimize exposure window)
   704→3. **Log all prompts locally** (your encrypted infrastructure)
   705→4. **Plan migration to self-hosted** (Year 2+, Iceland/Switzerland)
   706→5. **Never use AI for:**
   707→   - Real names of vulnerable individuals
   708→   - Immigration status information
   709→   - Legal strategy discussions (**no attorney-client privilege** — confirmed by federal court)
   710→   - Medical records (HIPAA violations)
   711→   - Financial data (PCI-DSS violations)
   712→6. **Prefer Anthropic over OpenAI** for values alignment (refused Pentagon weapons contract; OpenAI accepted)
   713→7. **Monitor FISA Section 702 renewal** (expires April 2026 — could expand or limit surveillance powers)
   714→
   715→**Nicholas Merrill's Legacy:** "You can't encrypt your way out of bad infrastructure. You have to design privacy into the infrastructure itself."
   716→
   717→**For Common Cloud:** Start with Claude Opus 4.6 API (Commercial Terms) for speed and values alignment; add Vertex AI Gemini for EU residency; plan migration to self-hosted Llama 4 / Mistral 3 / DeepSeek on Iceland servers for maximum sovereignty.

# AI MODEL SELECTION: Privacy & Threat Analysis (March 2026 Update)

**Date:** March 6, 2026
**Supersedes:** December 8, 2025 edition
**Context:** Common Cloud deployment for vulnerable communities (NSL/subpoena threat model)

---

## WHAT CHANGED SINCE DECEMBER 2025

Since the December 2025 edition of this document:

- **New models:** Claude Opus 4.6 & Sonnet 4.6 (Feb 2026), OpenAI GPT-5.4 (Mar 5, 2026), Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview (Feb 2026), Llama 4 family (Spring 2025), Mistral 3 family (Dec 2025)
- **Major court ruling:** US v. Heppner (Feb 2026) — AI chats have **no attorney-client privilege**
- **NYT v. OpenAI escalation:** Judge ordered OpenAI to turn over **20 million ChatGPT logs** (Jan 2026)
- **Anthropic $1.5B settlement:** Bartz v. Anthropic class action over pirated training data (Aug 2025)
- **DeepSeek regulatory storm:** Banned in Italy and South Korea; banned on US federal and multiple state government devices; third-party analysis found data sharing with China Mobile
- **OpenAI-Pentagon deal:** OpenAI contracted for classified Pentagon AI networks (Feb-Mar 2026); Anthropic was blacklisted for refusing to allow weapons/surveillance use
- **GDPR enforcement:** Italy fined OpenAI €15M (Dec 2024), fined Replika €5M (May 2025), banned DeepSeek (Jan 2025)
- **Privacy policy updates:** Anthropic (Jan 2026), OpenAI DPA (Jan 2026); Anthropic 5-year retention still in effect for opted-in consumers
- **New state AI laws:** California SB 53 and AB 2013, Texas RAIGA — all effective Jan 1, 2026

### Current Model Lineup (March 2026)

| Provider | Flagship Model | Released |
|----------|---------------|----------|
| Anthropic | **Claude Opus 4.6** | Feb 5, 2026 |
| Anthropic | **Claude Sonnet 4.6** | Feb 17, 2026 |
| Anthropic | **Claude Haiku 4.5** | Oct 1, 2025 |
| OpenAI | **GPT-5.4** | Mar 5, 2026 |
| OpenAI | **o3-pro / o4-mini** | 2025 |
| Google | **Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview** | Feb 2026 |
| Google | **Gemini 3 Flash Preview** | Jan 2026 |
| DeepSeek | **DeepSeek V3.1** | Aug 21, 2025 |
| Meta | **Llama 4 Scout / Maverick / Behemoth** | Spring 2025 |
| Mistral | **Mistral Large 3 / Ministral 3** | Dec 2025 |

---

## PART 1: MARCH 2026 MODEL LANDSCAPE

### **OpenAI GPT-5.4 / GPT-5.2 / o3-pro**

**Release Dates:**
- GPT-5.4: March 5, 2026 (latest flagship)
- GPT-5.2: December 2025
- GPT-5.1: November 2025
- o3-pro / o4-mini: 2025 (reasoning models)

**Key Features (GPT-5.4):**
- **1M context window**
- **Native computer-use:** First OpenAI model with built-in computer interaction
- **Pro tier:** Higher-quality output at premium pricing
- **GPT-5-mini and GPT-5-nano** available for budget deployments

**API Pricing (per 1M tokens):**

| Model | Input | Output |
|-------|-------|--------|
| GPT-5.4 | $2.50 | $15.00 |
| GPT-5.4 Pro | $30.00 | $180.00 |
| GPT-5.2 | $1.75 | $14.00 |
| GPT-5.1 | $1.25 | $10.00 |
| GPT-5-mini | $0.25 | $2.00 |
| GPT-5-nano | $0.05 | $0.40 |
| o3-pro | $20.00 | $80.00 |
| o3 | $2.00 | $8.00 |
| o4-mini | $1.10 | $4.40 |

**Consumer Plans:**
- ChatGPT Plus: $20/mo
- ChatGPT Pro: $200/mo (includes GPT-5.4 Pro)

---

### **Claude Opus 4.6 / Sonnet 4.6 / Haiku 4.5**

**Release Dates:**
- Opus 4.6: February 5, 2026
- Sonnet 4.6: February 17, 2026
- Haiku 4.5: October 1, 2025 (no Haiku 4.6 yet)
- Legacy: Opus 4.5 (Nov 2025), Sonnet 4.5 (Sept 2025) still available

**Key Features:**
- **Opus 4.6:** Most intelligent model; best for agents and complex coding. 200K context (1M beta). Extended thinking + adaptive thinking.
- **Sonnet 4.6:** Everyday workhorse; reportedly outperforms Opus on some real-world office tasks. 200K context (1M beta).
- **Haiku 4.5:** Fastest, lowest cost. 200K context.

**API Pricing (per 1M tokens):**

| Claude Sonnet 4.6 | $3.00 | $15.00 |
| Claude Haiku 4.5 | $1.00 | $5.00 |

**Availability:**
- Claude.ai (Pro, Max, Team Premium users)
- Claude Code (Pro users)
- API (anthropic.com)
- Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI

---

### **Google Gemini 3.1 Pro / Gemini 3 Flash**

**Release Dates:**
- Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview: February 2026
- Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite Preview: February 2026
- Gemini 3 Flash Preview: January 2026
- Gemini 2.5 Pro/Flash: Stable (2025)

**Key Features (Gemini 3.1 Pro):**
- **77.1% ARC-AGI-2 score**
